title: The Stages of Sleep
content: Sleep consists of four stages: N1, N2, N3 (deep sleep), and REM. Each stage plays a critical role in physical and mental restoration.

title: Circadian Rhythm 102
content: Your internal 24-hour clock regulates sleep-wake cycles, primarily influenced by light exposure and temperature.

title: Melatonin: The Darkness Hormone
content: Melatonin is produced by the pineal gland in response to darkness, signaling to your body that it's time to rest.

title: The Impact of Caffeine
content: Caffeine blocks adenosine receptors in the brain, which normally build up 'sleep pressure' throughout the day.

title: Blue Light and Sleep
content: Exposure to blue light from screens suppresses melatonin production, making it harder to fall asleep at night.

title: The Role of Magnesium
content: Magnesium helps regulate neurotransmitters like GABA, which calms the nervous system and prepares you for sleep.

title: Sleep Hygiene Basics
content: Consistent schedules, a cool room, and a dark environment are the pillars of good sleep hygiene.

title: REM Sleep and Memory
content: Rapid Eye Movement (REM) sleep is crucial for emotional processing and memory consolidation.

title: Understanding Sleep Apnea
content: Sleep apnea involves repeated pauses in breathing during sleep, leading to fragmented rest and health risks.

title: The Power Nap
content: A 10-20 minute nap can boost alertness and mood without causing 'sleep inertia' from waking up during deep sleep.

title: Temperature and Sleep
content: The body needs to drop its core temperature by about 2-3 degrees Fahrenheit to initiate sleep effectively.

title: Alcohol and Sleep Architecture
content: While alcohol may help you fall asleep faster, it severely fragments sleep and suppresses REM sleep later in the night.

title: The Adenosine Cycle
content: Adenosine is a chemical that builds up in your brain the longer you are awake, creating a natural urge to sleep.

title: Stress and Cortisol
content: High cortisol levels from stress keep the body in a state of high alert, directly opposing the relaxation needed for sleep.

title: Chronotypes Explained
content: Whether you are a 'morning lark' or 'night owl' is largely determined by genetics and your individual circadian rhythm.

title: Sleep and Immune Function
content: During sleep, your immune system releases proteins called cytokines, which help the body fight off infections.

title: The Dangers of Sleep Deprivation
content: Lack of sleep impairs judgment, reaction time, and increases the risk of chronic diseases like diabetes and heart disease.

title: The Benefits of a Bedtime Routine
content: A consistent pre-sleep routine signals to the brain that it's time to wind down, reducing sleep onset latency.

title: Exercise and Sleep Quality
content: Regular aerobic exercise increases the amount of deep sleep you get, though intense workouts late at night can be disruptive.

title: Lucid Dreaming
content: Lucid dreaming occurs when you become aware that you are dreaming during REM sleep, allowing some level of control.
